mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI or C250d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM651 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only; a reconditioned OM654 costs £3,500–£7,500. Used units are available from £1,500–£3,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your C-Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI / C250d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 is a proven and torquey engine, but it has well-documented weaknesses: timing chain stretch, injector seal leaks, and intake manifold bolt failure. The OM654 is more modern and efficient but suffers from rocker arm bearing wear and timing chain issues. Both engines demand diligent maintenance with correct MB 229.51 spec oil.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI and C250d?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651: timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start), injector seal failure (diesel smell), intake manifold bolt failure (boost leaks, limp mode), DPF pressure sensor failure. OM654: rocker arm bearing wear (tapping noises, potential camshaft damage), timing chain wear, high-pressure fuel pump failure. The OM651's timing chain is the most critical concern.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI / C250d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: C-Class values remain strong — a well-kept W205 is often worth £10,000–£20,000+. A £2,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork and interior, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI or C250d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W204 (2008–2014) uses OM651.911/912; W205 (2014–2018) uses OM651.911; W205 facelift (2018–2021) uses OM654. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner can be replaced preventatively — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,500+). At the first sign of rattling from the front of the engine on an OM651, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI / C250d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current C250 market. OM651-engined cars sit at the lower end of the range; OM654 engines command a premium. The OM651 is a well-understood engine with good parts availability, while the OM654 is more complex and expensive to rebuild.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C250 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM651's transverse (W204) or longitudinal (W205) layout affects labour times.

cta.label: → Compare C250 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch and Guide Wear (OM651)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, delayed starting, engine warning light, cam timing errors
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the timing chain fails on the OM651, piston-valve contact is likely

problems.recommendation: The OM651 is chain-driven, which is an advantage over belt-driven rivals. However, timing chain stretch is a known issue. If you hear rattling from the front of the engine on cold start, have the timing chain inspected immediately. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct MB 229.51 spec oil are essential — one owner reported timing chain stretch after just 78,000 miles.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Injector Seal and Fuel Injector Failure (OM651)
problems.symptoms: Diesel smell inside or around the car, rough idle, knocking noise when cold, hard starting, loss of power, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 (injector seal replacement or full injector replacement)
problems.recommendation: Early OM651 engines can suffer from injector failure due to worn copper seals. Leaking injector seals allow combustion gases to escape, causing a knocking noise when cold and a diesel smell inside the car. If you notice a diesel smell or rough running, have the injector seals checked promptly. Catching seal leaks early prevents more serious damage. One owner reported injector failure at just 3,000 miles.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector seal failure guide

problems.title: Intake Manifold Bolt Failure and Boost Leaks (OM651)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, low boost, engine warning light (P0299, P2279), DPF soot accumulation, limp mode, whooshing sound under acceleration
problems.recommendation: The OM651 has a well-documented issue with sheared bolts on the intake manifold. A broken bolt can cause a boost leak, leading to low boost codes (P0299), air intake leak codes (P2279), and DPF soot accumulation because the car won't regenerate while the check engine light is on. If you experience loss of power or hear a whooshing sound under acceleration, have the intake manifold inspected. Some owners report that replacing the manifold and fixing the bolt shearing issue resolves the problem.

failureLink.label: Read the full intake manifold failure guide

problems.title: Rocker Arm Bearing Wear (OM654)
problems.symptoms: Tapping or ticking noise from the top of the engine, reduced power, odd noises, excess exhaust smoke, engine warning light

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: worn rocker arm bearings can lead to catastrophic camshaft damage

problems.recommendation: The OM654 has a known issue with failing rocker arm bearings. Symptoms include odd noises and excess exhaust smoke. If left unchecked, rocker arm bearing failure can lead to camshaft damage, requiring extensive and expensive repairs. If you hear tapping or ticking from the top of the engine, have the valvetrain inspected immediately. A preventative rocker arm replacement is far cheaper than a camshaft replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full valvetrain failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C250 CDI / C250d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Well-understood repair — catch before further damage
Replace manifold — restore boost and performance
Labour-intensive but cheaper than a camshaft replacement
Simple sensor replacement — restore DPF function

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C250 CDI / C250d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2018 OM654 with comprehensive warranty — the newer engine is more refined but complex
* OM651 with documented timing chain work — the single most important check on the 2.1-litre
* Evidence of injector service or replacement — a common wear item
* Full service history with correct MB 229.51 spec oil — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of DPF maintenance and regular motorway driving
avoidIf: * Any rattling from the front of the engine — timing chain wear on OM651 is likely
* Diesel smell inside the car — injector seal leaks are expensive to fix
* OM654 with tapping from the top of the engine — rocker arm bearing wear is likely
* Poor service history — both engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Any "check engine" light without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C250 CDI and C250d are powered by two of Mercedes' finest four-cylinder diesels. The OM651 (2008–2018) is a proven workhorse — torquey, refined and capable of high mileage — but timing chain stretch, injector seal failure and intake manifold bolt issues are well-documented. The OM654 (2018–2021) is a more modern all-aluminium engine — efficient and smooth — but rocker arm bearing wear and timing chain issues are emerging concerns. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and both engines will reward you with effortless performance.

cta.label: → Compare C250 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz C250 CDI or C250d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles from the front of the engine (timing chain wear on OM651 — the most critical check)
2. Service History — including which generation (W204 or W205) and evidence of timing chain work
3. Diesel Smell — Any smell inside the car? (injector seal leak warning)
4. Boost Performance — Any loss of power or whooshing sound? (intake manifold bolt failure)
5. Top-End Ticking (OM654) — Any tapping from the top of the engine? (rocker arm bearing wear)
6. DPF Health — Any warning lights or reduced power? (DPF pressure sensor failure)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — two engine families across the C250's history.
